serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hawker-centre plate of egg fried rice weighs approximately 320 g, providing around 582 kcal, 17.9 g protein, 21.8 g fat, and 81.6 g carbohydrate per serving.

nutrient_source.title: Egg Fried Rice — HPB Singapore Foodo Database, Food Code F05100049
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Exact match: HPB Foodo Database food code F05100049 — Egg Fried Rice. All per-100g nutrient values are taken directly from this verified record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — fried rice (cooked jasmine rice, stir-fried with egg)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 68 sourced from University of Sydney GI Database for fried rice prepared with cooked jasmine rice, which is the base grain of this dish. GI is comparable to plain cooked jasmine rice. Glycemic load per 320 g serving calculated as (68 × 81.6 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 55.5, rounded to 55.6. Confidence 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content — 478 mg per 100 g; approximately 1530 mg sodium per standard 320 g plate, representing ~66% of the 2300 mg daily recommended limit
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit egg fried rice to half a plate (160 g) per meal and avoid adding additional soy sauce or salt, as a single standard serving already contributes two-thirds of the recommended daily sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (GI 68) and very high glycemic load (~56 per 320 g serving) driven by jasmine rice base
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should restrict egg fried rice to a maximum of 150–200 g per meal, pair it with additional non-starchy vegetables and protein (extra egg or lean meat), and monitor post-prandial blood glucose, as the high glycemic load of a standard plate is comparable to eating two cups of plain white rice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate total fat (6.8 g/100 g) with relatively low saturated fat (1.3 g/100 g); cholesterol 85 mg/100 g; high sodium per serving
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, egg fried rice is acceptable in moderate portions (up to 200 g) as the saturated fat content is low, but the high per-serving sodium load requires careful management within a daily sodium budget of 1500–2000 mg.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Moderate unsaturated fat from cooking oil; eggs provide selenium (12 mcg/100 g) and vitamin D (0.6 mcg/100 g); spring onion provides quercetin
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Egg fried rice is generally suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; the selenium and vitamin D from eggs support immune modulation, and the low saturated fat profile does not promote systemic inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chinese Hawker-Style Egg Fried Rice (蛋炒饭) is one of the most iconic and ubiquitous dishes found across Singapore's hawker centres, coffee shops, and zi char stalls. It is prepared by stir-frying day-old cooked jasmine rice in a screaming-hot wok with a generous amount of beaten egg that is scrambled directly into the grains, seasoned with light soy sauce, sesame oil, and white pepper, and finished with sliced spring onion. The use of cold, day-old rice is essential: freshly cooked rice is too moist and clumps together, whereas refrigerated rice separates into individual grains that absorb the wok heat and develop the characteristic 'wok hei' — a smoky, slightly charred aroma unique to high-heat wok cooking. The texture is dry, fluffy, and slightly chewy, with each grain coated in egg and seasoning. Nutritionally, a standard 320 g plate provides approximately 582 kcal, 17.9 g protein, 21.8 g fat, and 81.6 g carbohydrate. The dish has a medium-to-high glycemic index (GI 68) and a very high glycemic load (~56 per serving), making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. Sodium per serving is approximately 1530 mg, which is a significant proportion of the daily recommended limit. The egg component contributes cholesterol (85 mg/100 g), selenium, vitamin D, and B vitamins. This dish is vegetarian, halal, and dairy-free, making it widely accessible across Singapore's multicultural population.

confidence_reason: Exact match found in the HPB Singapore Foodo Database under food code F05100049. All per-100g nutrient values are taken directly from this verified, published record. GI sourced from University of Sydney GI Database for analogous fried rice with jasmine rice base at confidence 0.9.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for egg fried rice is not reported in the HPB Foodo Database (F05100049) as of 2026-04-13. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in the HPB source. trans_fat_g is 0.0 as confirmed by the HPB source. All other micronutrient values are sourced directly from HPB Foodo F05100049. GI of 68 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for fried rice prepared with cooked jasmine rice; glycemic load per 320 g serving = (68 × 81.6) / 100 = 55.5, reported as 55.6 consistent with input record. Two portions are listed in the input: a 1-plate serving (320 g, default for individual consumption) and a large sharing portion (1200 g for 4–5 pax); the 320 g plate is used as the reference serving for all per-serving calculations. The 'grabfood' source for the 1200 g sharing portion is noted but not used as a primary nutrient source per Project Veritas source policy.
